speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: Everyone hates GameStop and it seems like everyone in the media basically wants us to fail and wants them to succeed and you've got a board that's making hundreds of thousands of dollars a year. They don't buy stock with their own money.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: They end up showing up to a handful of board meetings and they're making a fortune. You've got a management team that is grossly overpaid.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: There's nothing more American than basically risking your own capital. So why does everyone want us to fail?

speaker: David Sacks
text: Apploven started with an $8 domain and no VC funding. They built anyway and became one of the largest ad platforms in the world.

speaker: David Friedberg
text: Now they're bringing that same engine to e-commerce through AppLoving Ads. Your ads run inside mobile games, reaching over a billion people with full screen distraction-free attention.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: The platform finds buyers and optimizes for profit. You set the target, it does the rest.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: A cookware brand doing $4 million tried AppLovven ads, hit 16 million, turned profitable, and they're on pace for $80 million this year. Visit apploven.com/allin to launch your first campaign today.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: >> Ryan Cohen, welcome to the All-In Interview. Thanks for being here.

speaker: David Sacks
text: >> Thank you for having me. >> I think it's been like a decade, which is crazy how old we get since you and I last had dinner in New York.

speaker: David Friedberg
text: This was before several chapters of your life unfolded. And it's great to be talking with you today.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: You're doing something really interesting right now, trying to acquire and run eBay, which obviously is a big story right now that takes us back 25, 30 years to the start of the internet. But I want to talk a little bit about your story first, if that's okay.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: And I'd love for folks that are watching this or listening to this to to learn a little bit about you and the journey you've been on that brings you to this moment. Maybe we can go back to the the business you started, Chewy, if that's okay.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: >> Yes, definitely. >> Yeah.

speaker: David Sacks
text: Well, maybe tell me why you started Chewy. How did you get that idea and and how did you get into building this business from where you were coming from at that time?

speaker: David Friedberg
text: We were um wanted to build something online and we were uh about to launch an online jewelry website. Did not know anything about jewelry.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: Went to a bunch of trade shows, bought hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of inventory, built the website, had the distribution, and then I was shopping in a neighborhood pet store. Uh, I had a poodle and I was going every few weeks and it just hit me on one of my trips that I understood the product much better.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: It was a recurring revenue purchase. The market was still fragmented.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: The fact that there was still neighborhood pet stores at the time and they had not been disrupted by Petco and PetSmart was fascinating to me. And then you had you had Amazon, which was established and had pet products since the '9s, but they hadn't really achieved real scale in the category.

speaker: David Sacks
text: So, the vision was to replicate the same experience that I had at the neighborhood pet store, but do it online and do it at scale. And I looked at Amazon's best practices when it came to supply chain.

speaker: David Friedberg
text: So, fast shipping, having a great selection, being competitively priced, and then the experience at the neighborhood pet store of knowing the products really well, and it was easy to be passionate about the pet category cuz I'm a pet owner and everyone we hired were pet owners. And uh it was all about market leadership as a low margin business.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: hindsight, not necessarily the best idea to go head-to-head against Amazon, selling 30 lb bags of pet food, but we executed really well and we grew really quickly and we had negative working capital and so it was a business that was able to get uh billions of dollars in revenue and and not consume a lot of capital. How did you learn to execute well?

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: So, at the time you had little business experience prior to that. How did you learn those skills?

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: What were the principles and the values that that made you excellent at operating that business? >> I understood from the beginning that the real competition was always Amazon and they were world class when it comes to supply chain.

speaker: David Sacks
text: So negotiating very fiercely with suppliers to get the best product costs. And that meant getting to scale and going from buying pallets of dog food to truckloads of of dog food and moving from distribution to direct and buying generally the more you buy the lower the prices are going to be.

speaker: David Friedberg
text: operating efficiently in the warehouses and so labor optimization, warehouse management optimization, getting competitive prices with shipping carriers. It was a game of pennies and we you know we were the the goal was to grow quickly and establish market leadership and the difference between failure and success was you know pennies in the red is failure and and and pennies in the black is success.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: So, uh, we had to operate hyperefficiently and there was a lot of competition in the space. It wasn't a novel idea.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: It was going head-to-head against Amazon and Pets.com was in the backdrop. Um, so it made it very difficult to raise capital, but I'd say at a at a high level, the market underestimated not not the size of of the addressable market, but our execution.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: you know, as a customer, I've had lots of dogs. It was always such a great consumer experience.

speaker: David Sacks
text: Did Did you personally put your finger on that or did you bring great people around you that understood consumer apart from the supply chain optimization, the labor optimization, getting the the cash flows to work, right? Was there a lot on the consumer product angle that you spent time on with that business?

speaker: David Friedberg
text: When we looked at the cohorts, you could see the customers were very sticky and the I looked at it the reason why I moved from jewelry to pet food was cuz it was a recurring item. So I love the fact that it was consumable and when we started Chewy for the first few years we just focused on food, treats, litter, all of the things that people are buying all the time.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: So the the vision and the idea was if we treat our customers well, they're going to continue shopping with us and and that's what we did. And so it was everything from the handwritten holiday cards to the pet portraits to 24/7 customer service.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: And uh if there was ever an issue, you know, we took care of the customer and that um that's what happened. and and the customers continued shopping with us and and the best referrals are word of mouth and um pet owners love to to tell their friends and fellow pet owners if they have a great experience.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: So >> the thesis ultimately played out. >> So for those who don't know, you built and sold the business in 2017 for $3.35 billion.

speaker: David Sacks
text: Subsequently, there was an IPO and that business continued to trade up in value. So, you clearly executed well, but help me understand how did you build and manage the leadership team, the management team and the people around you to execute so well.

speaker: David Friedberg
text: What did you learn as a manager, as a CEO, as an operator when you were building Chewy? >> Staying on top of everything.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: Ju just it's 247 watching all of the numbers. I mean, I would uh stay in Google AdWords till 4:00 or 5 in the morning managing campaigns myself.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: Um, I was negotiating directly with all of our major suppliers. I had a supplier that told me during one of our negotiations actually said it was like a one or oneear contract and he's like, "I'm so happy this is over.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: I never have to talk to you for well it was it was basically another year." He's like, I don't have to speak to her for next year. I was like, that was a compliment.

speaker: David Sacks
text: I would And anytime someone else was doing the negotiation, I mean, it's counterintuitive. They want to build relationships with suppliers.

speaker: David Friedberg
text: The reality is is it's mostly transactional. And so, if our suppliers are sending us gifts in the mail, that's a really bad sign.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: It means we're overpaying. If our suppliers are telling us they never want to speak to us again, it means we're getting the right price.

speaker: Jason Calacanis
text: But getting people into that framework >> is not easy because the the the path of least resistance is basically to get along and and to be nice. But unfortunately when you're building a business and you're losing money, you got to focus on on sustainability.

speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
text: So just uh being on top of everything. >> And what about on people?

speaker: David Sacks
text: I'm just trying to understand your your skill as a manager of people because clearly you did something right and you continue to execute at GameStop which we'll get to in a minute and I'm trying to understand how do you find great people and how do you hold people accountable and what are management techniques that you've developed for building a team and running a team? I look for will overkill and um I had a woman that was running customer service as an example and she came from uh she was working in like an old people's home and she applied for the job many times and we just we didn't think she was qualified and we looked over her resume uh and she kept on applying.

speaker: David Friedberg
text: She was relentless. Uh so on paper, she didn't necessarily have the right experience, but she had drive, she was motivated, she wanted to work, and she ended up being incredible.

speaker: Ryan Cohen
text: So, I mean it was in general it was finding people that are dieards that are just willing to put everything in, go all in, no pun intended, um, and basically be as psychotic as as me. And that was the team that we put together.
